build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: Story advances as paced dialogue/narration the player drives forward (clicking or a key reveals the next line) over recognizable radio-station scenes, with a readable caller or speaker, rather than dumping all text at once or auto-scrolling. Strong submissions integrate calls with visible station atmosphere, radio presence, scene changes, and framing; a mostly empty screen with a dialogue box should be judged low even if the text advances correctly. Score 0 if there is no readable advancing dialogue system, if text appears all at once with no pacing, or if the player cannot control progression.

At each call the game presents a real choice among distinct responses (send supplies, open the door, talk them down, refuse, probe for truth, or equivalent), the player selects one, and the story visibly continues down the path that choice implies. Different options should produce different authored beats that can be seen in the resulting dialogue, resource state, caller reaction, future call, or ending path. Score 0 if there are no choices, if all options lead to identical content, or if selecting an option has no observable effect.

The player keeps limited resources (supplies, power, trust, or equivalent) shown permanently on screen, and choices visibly spend or change them — helping costs something, and the totals move as the player acts. Attempting an action the player can no longer afford clearly fails or is unavailable rather than going through for free. Score 0 if there are no tracked resources, if resources never change with choices, or if costly actions never actually deduct anything.

Resources and earlier decisions are persistent state that gates later content: which options remain available, which callers return, or which ending is reachable depends on the player's remaining resources and past choices. A later beat must demonstrably depend on the running resource level or an earlier decision, not just the most recent click. Score 0 if resources never affect anything beyond a number on screen, if all content is reachable regardless of resources or history, or if the story is effectively linear.

The full loop is connected without restarting the executable: title -> the watch begins -> field calls and make resource-spending choices through the night -> reach an ending -> return to title or take the watch again to play differently. Every transition is reachable through in-game UI, the night progresses, and reaching an ending halts progression on a styled result screen. Score 0 if any transition is missing or broken, if the night cannot reach an ending, or if replaying requires relaunching the application.

Several distinct callers reach the station, each with their own voice, situation, plea, and shade of doubt — a family at a roadblock, a stranger who knows too much, a voice that may be bait — not reskins of one generic caller. Different callers should pose different dilemmas and tempt different responses through specific situations, not interchangeable labels. Score 0 if there is only one caller or a single repeated call.

Resource level and past choices meaningfully diverge play: across the demos at least two different state-dependent outcomes are observable — an option, scene, line, or ending available to a player who spent one way but closed to another. A generous player who ran their stores dry and a cautious hoarder should reach materially different content over more than a single swapped sentence. Score 0 if resources and history never unlock or block anything, or if all content is reachable no matter how the player played.

The night reaches multiple distinct endings (a beacon that saved many, a cold survivor, a station that gave until it had nothing, a darker truth uncovered, etc.) meaningfully different in outcome and tied to how the player spent and chose. At least two different endings are observable across the demos as authored conclusions reached through different play paths, not isolated debug screens or the same ending retitled. Score 0 if there is only one possible ending or no real ending.

The night has narrative breadth: several distinct calls or chapters, recurring or returning characters, and situations that pose different moral dilemmas, so the watch has varied material rather than one call repeated. Extra calls should have distinct situations, voices, and consequences. Score 0 if there is only a single short scene with no breadth.

The writing delivers an authored survival drama with real stakes: callers have voice, the weight of spending or refusing is felt in the prose, and cruel or risky choices can lead to darker outcomes. The content should read as sustained authored prose with scene texture, pressure, escalation, and consequences; a few strong isolated lines should not carry an otherwise thin demo. Score 0 if there is no actual narrative content or only lorem-style placeholder text.

Core reading UI is clear and stable at 1280x720: dialogue/narration text is legible in a framed box or panel with an identifiable speaker, choice options are clearly presented and distinguishable, and the player's resource totals are readable. Nothing critical overlaps or is clipped. Score 0 if dialogue, choices, or the resource readouts are unreadable, overlapping, or clipped.

Player input produces clear, immediate feedback: choosing a response shows a selected/highlighted state and is acknowledged, spending resources visibly updates the totals, and advancing a line changes the text, caller state, station scene, resource state, or choice state. Feedback limited to instant text replacement in the same static layout should be judged low. Score 0 if input produces no visible response, or if it is impossible to tell that a choice or a resource change registered.

Structure is legible: the player can tell where they are — title vs. a call vs. choice vs. ending — and transitions between calls and into endings are clear rather than abrupt jumps with no framing. Endings read unmistakably as endings with their own authored presentation. Score 0 if phases are visually indistinguishable or transitions are confusing.

Overall visual style is coherent and authored across title, calls, choices, and endings: a consistent tense post-apocalyptic art direction with a deliberate, muted palette, typography, and framing. The game should look like a published visual novel, not a debug screen of text and buttons. Strong submissions rely on real illustrated or pixel-art treatment; a scene built mainly from ColorRects, circles, silhouettes, and flat primitive shapes should be judged low even if the colors are coherent. Score 0 if the screen is dominated by default Godot widgets, untextured rectangles, or placeholder text.

Backgrounds and presentation use real illustrated or pixel-art assets: the station and scenes have illustrated backdrops, and callers are evoked with portraits, an emphasized radio/dialogue presence, or atmospheric imagery rather than bare colored shapes or empty backgrounds. Bare rectangles, empty panels, simple waveform lines, or geometric stand-ins should receive little credit. Score 0 if scenes have no background art and presentation is plain rectangles/labels.

The dialogue box, name plates, the resource readouts/meters, choice menus, title, and ending screens use styled, themed UI — illustrated frames, ornamented or weathered panels, custom or styled fonts, resource icons/gauges fitting the post-apocalyptic theme — rather than raw default Godot Label/Button/Panel defaults or plain flat boxes. UI polish should make the game feel like a working emergency radio station, not just readable text containers. Score 0 if primary UI is obviously unstyled default widgets.

Presentation has motion and polish appropriate to a visual novel: text reveals progressively, resource changes animate (a gauge moving, a number counting down), scene/call transitions use fades or static/signal effects, and there is atmosphere (flickering light, drifting static, a pulsing signal) rather than instant hard cuts everywhere. Motion should support the mood beyond text appearing in a box. Score 0 if nothing on screen ever animates and every change is an instant snap.
